blurb: A vigorous riparian native legume with airy, ferny pinnate foliage and dramatic spikes of tiny deep-purple flowers lit by glowing orange anthers in early summer. A nitrogen-fixing pollinator plant for tough or wet ground — but a determined spreader that needs a firm hand near waterways.
water: Adaptable — thrives in moist, streamside ground but takes ordinary garden water or drought once established
spread: Spreads by seed and root suckers into dense thickets — can be weedy along waterways, so contain it
seasons: Deep purple-and-orange flower spikes in late spring to early summer; airy pinnate foliage yellowing in fall; bumpy brown seed pods persisting into winter
wildlife: A nitrogen-fixing legume that draws nectar bees and butterflies; a larval host for several butterflies and skippers; seeds are eaten by birds
toxic: Not a food plant — the seeds and foliage carry mildly toxic compounds

care.hardiness: Cold-hardy through roughly USDA zones 4a–9b, so Front-Range winters are easy for it; it is native across much of North America including Colorado, where it grows wild along stream and pond edges, gravel bars and canyon bottoms.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to part shade; it is remarkably unfussy about site. Because it seeds and suckers freely, choose the spot deliberately — it is superb for erosion control on a raw bank or detention basin, but a poor choice crowded into a small bed or set loose next to a natural waterway. In pots, give it a big container and shelter it over its first winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Flowers hardest and grows densest in full sun; in part shade it grows leggier, with foliage concentrated toward the top.[1][2]
care.soil: Tolerates clay, loam and sand across a wide pH range; rated a facultative wetland plant, it handles everything from moist, periodically wet ground to dry sand.[1][2]
care.water: Very adaptable water-wise — it thrives streamside and takes occasional flooding, yet is drought-tolerant once its deep roots are down, so it fits both wet spots and ordinary Front-Range beds.[1][2]
care.spacing: Give it 6–10 ft of room; it is a fast, multi-stemmed, spreading shrub whose width often exceeds its height, and it will colonize outward from suckers.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easy from seed (the hard-coated seed benefits from scarification and a short cold-moist stratification), and also from softwood or root cuttings; it self-sows readily and can be divided from suckers.[1]
care.bloom: Narrow, spike-like racemes 3–8 in long crowd the branch tips in late spring to early summer, each packed with small single-petaled purple-violet flowers whose protruding orange-gold anthers give the whole spike a glowing look; bumpy, warty little seed pods follow and hang on into winter.[1][2]
care.pruning: Prune in late winter while dormant. It blooms on the current season's growth and resprouts vigorously, so it takes hard renewal well — cut a leggy, bare-bottomed plant back low to force dense new shoots. The bigger pruning job is containment: cut and pull the outer root suckers each year, and shear off the spent flower spikes before the pods ripen if you want to curb its heavy self-seeding.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low care once sited, but its spread is the ongoing task. Deadhead or trim the seed spikes before they shatter, and mow, pull or cut the suckers at the colony's edge — especially anywhere near a stream, ditch or wild area, where its seed and suckers can make it weedy.[1][2]

edible.summary: Not a food plant — no part is eaten, and the seeds and foliage carry mildly toxic compounds.[1]
edible.caution: False indigo bush is grown as an ornamental and pollinator plant, not for food. Its seeds and foliage contain bitter, mildly toxic glycoside compounds and are not eaten; historically related Amorpha species were used as insecticidal, not culinary, plants. Keep it off the plate.[1]
